content.body: Diffusion is the net movement of particles from a region of high concentration to a region of low concentration, down a concentration gradient. It occurs in both gases and liquids, and requires NO energy (passive process). Diffusion is vital for gas exchange in lungs, oxygen entering cells, and CO₂ leaving cells.

Key terms:
Concentration gradient: difference in concentration between two regions
Net movement: overall direction of diffusion (high → low)
Equilibrium: when concentrations are equal — particles still move but NET movement = 0

Rate of diffusion is faster when:
• Concentration gradient is steeper (bigger difference)
• Temperature is higher (particles have more energy)
• Distance is shorter
• Surface area is larger
• Particles are smaller

content.title: Examples of Diffusion
content.body:
1. In the lungs (alveoli):
   • O₂ diffuses from alveoli (high O₂) → blood (low O₂)
   • CO₂ diffuses from blood (high CO₂) → alveoli (low CO₂)

2. In body cells:
   • O₂ diffuses from blood → cells (used in respiration)
   • CO₂ diffuses from cells → blood

3. Smell of perfume spreading across a room
4. Food colouring spreading in water
5. Glucose from gut lumen → blood

Adaptations for efficient diffusion in the lungs:
• Alveoli: large total surface area (~70 m² in humans!)
• Alveoli walls: very thin (one cell thick) — short diffusion distance
• Rich capillary blood supply: maintains steep concentration gradient
• Alveoli are moist — gases dissolve for diffusion

Fick's Law: Rate of diffusion ∝ (surface area × concentration difference) / thickness of membrane

questions.question: Diffusion is the movement of particles from:
questions.answer: High to low concentration
questions.explanation: Diffusion is the net movement of particles down the concentration gradient (from high to low concentration).

questions.question: Does diffusion require energy?
options: No, it is a passive process
questions.answer: No, it is a passive process
questions.explanation: Diffusion is passive — it requires no energy input. Particles move spontaneously down the concentration gradient.

questions.question: In the lungs, O₂ diffuses from:
questions.answer: Alveoli to blood
questions.explanation: Alveoli have high O₂ (from inhaled air). Blood returning from the body has low O₂. So O₂ diffuses from alveoli → blood.

questions.question: What makes alveoli efficient for diffusion?
options: Large surface area, thin walls, rich blood supply
They are connected to the heart
questions.answer: Large surface area, thin walls, rich blood supply
questions.explanation: These three features maximise diffusion rate according to Fick's Law.

questions.question: Diffusion rate increases if:
questions.answer: Surface area increases
questions.explanation: Greater surface area provides more space for diffusion to occur — increasing the rate.

questions.question: When diffusion equilibrium is reached:
options: Net movement stops but particles still move randomly
All particles move to one side
questions.answer: Net movement stops but particles still move randomly
questions.explanation: At equilibrium, concentrations are equal. Particles still move randomly, but net directional movement is zero.

questions.question: Glucose absorbed in the small intestine enters the blood by:
options: Diffusion (from high conc. in gut to blood)
questions.answer: Diffusion (from high conc. in gut to blood)
questions.explanation: After digestion, glucose concentration in the gut lumen is high. It diffuses down its concentration gradient into the blood.

questions.question: Fick's Law states that diffusion rate is proportional to:
options: Surface area × concentration difference / membrane thickness
questions.answer: Surface area × concentration difference / membrane thickness
questions.explanation: Fick's Law: Rate ∝ (Surface area × concentration difference) / thickness of exchange membrane.

questions.question: Why does CO₂ diffuse from cells into the blood?
options: Blood has more CO₂ than cells
Cells have more CO₂ (from respiration) than blood
questions.answer: Cells have more CO₂ (from respiration) than blood
questions.explanation: Respiration produces CO₂ in cells, making cell CO₂ concentration high. Blood has low CO₂ — so CO₂ diffuses from cells into blood.

questions.question: The smell of cooking reaches all parts of a house. This is:
options: Diffusion of scent particles through air
questions.answer: Diffusion of scent particles through air
questions.explanation: Scent molecules diffuse from high concentration (kitchen) to low concentration (rest of house) — a real-world example of gaseous diffusion.

content.body: Osmosis is the special case of diffusion involving water. It is the net movement of water molecules from a region of high water concentration (dilute solution) to a region of low water concentration (concentrated solution) through a PARTIALLY PERMEABLE membrane. Osmosis is crucial for plant turgor pressure, kidney function, and cell volume control.

Partially permeable membrane:
• Allows water (small molecules) to pass through
• Does NOT allow solute (large molecules like glucose, starch) to pass through

Water potential:
• Pure water has highest water potential (ψ = 0)
• Adding solute LOWERS water potential
• Water moves from HIGH to LOW water potential (= high to low water concentration)

content.title: Osmosis in Plant Cells
content.body:
TURGID cell (healthy): water has entered by osmosis → cell is firm/swollen → cell wall prevents bursting (turgor pressure pushes outward)

FLACCID cell: cell has lost water → limp/wilted → lower turgor pressure

PLASMOLYSIS: cell in very concentrated solution → cell membrane pulls away from cell wall → cell cannot function

Plants in dry soil: soil is more concentrated → water moves OUT of roots by osmosis → plant wilts

content.title: Osmosis in Animal Cells
content.body:
In HYPOTONIC solution (more dilute than cell): water enters cell → cell SWELLS → may BURST (lysis)

In HYPERTONIC solution (more concentrated than cell): water leaves cell → cell SHRIVELS (crenation)

In ISOTONIC solution (same concentration as cell): no net movement → cell stays the same

Blood plasma is kept isotonic with red blood cells — this is why saline drips use 0.9% salt solution.

questions.question: Osmosis is the movement of:
options: Water molecules through a partially permeable membrane
questions.answer: Water molecules through a partially permeable membrane
questions.explanation: Osmosis is specifically the net movement of water through a partially permeable membrane.

questions.question: Water moves by osmosis from:
questions.answer: Dilute to concentrated solution
questions.explanation: Water moves from where it is in higher concentration (dilute solution) to lower concentration (concentrated solution).

questions.question: A turgid plant cell has:
options: Gained water and is firm due to turgor pressure
questions.answer: Gained water and is firm due to turgor pressure
questions.explanation: Turgid = swollen with water. Water has entered by osmosis, creating turgor pressure against the cell wall — keeping the plant firm.

questions.question: Plasmolysis occurs when:
options: Too much water enters a cell
Cell is placed in very concentrated solution — membrane pulls from wall
questions.answer: Cell is placed in very concentrated solution — membrane pulls from wall
questions.explanation: In hypertonic solution, water leaves by osmosis. The cell contents shrink so much the membrane peels away from the wall — plasmolysis.

questions.question: A red blood cell placed in distilled water will:
questions.answer: Swell and may burst
questions.explanation: Distilled water is hypotonic (more dilute than the cell contents). Water enters by osmosis, causing the cell to swell and potentially burst (lysis).

questions.question: Hospital saline drips use 0.9% NaCl because:
options: It is isotonic with blood — no osmosis disruption
questions.answer: It is isotonic with blood — no osmosis disruption
questions.explanation: 0.9% NaCl has the same solute concentration as blood plasma — no net osmosis occurs, so cells are not damaged.

questions.question: What passes through a partially permeable membrane?
options: Small water molecules freely; large solutes cannot pass
questions.answer: Small water molecules freely; large solutes cannot pass
questions.explanation: Partially permeable membranes allow small molecules (water) through but block large solute molecules like glucose and starch.

questions.question: Osmosis is a type of:
questions.answer: Special case of diffusion (water only)
questions.explanation: Osmosis is diffusion specifically of water molecules through a partially permeable membrane.

questions.question: A wilting plant can be restored by:
options: Watering it (water enters roots by osmosis)
questions.answer: Watering it (water enters roots by osmosis)
questions.explanation: Water in the soil is more dilute than cell contents. Water enters by osmosis restoring turgor pressure and making the plant firm again.

questions.question: Red blood cell placed in concentrated salt solution will:
questions.answer: Shrivel (crenation)
questions.explanation: Hypertonic solution has lower water potential than the cell. Water leaves by osmosis, causing the cell to shrivel — this is called crenation.
